summary: The post addresses a common misconception about service-oriented architecture: that SOA necessarily creates silos between development teams. Fournier argues that at smaller companies, SOA should enable 'internal open source' where developers work across services as needed to build features. She contrasts this with large company approaches where service ownership creates intentional boundaries for data governance. The key is to architect both your systems and your teams to avoid unintended separation, whether through cross-functional business-focused teams, microservices, or unified technology stacks.
key_points: SOA is not designed to separate developers from each other; systems should be treated as 'internal open source' where any team can contribute
Large companies may intentionally use SOA to create ownership boundaries for data quality, accepting velocity tradeoffs that startups cannot afford
Language differences between services (e.g., Java backend, Ruby frontend) naturally segregate teams, requiring deliberate counter-measures
Business-focused teams with both backend and frontend developers help overcome the separation problem
The 'separation myth' makes developers reluctant to adopt SOA, but proper team architecture can avoid this pitfall
money_quotes: SOA is not designed to separate your developers from each other.

The systems are 'internal open source'. You may not be the expert in that system, but when a feature is needed, it's expected that the team creating the feature will roll up their sleeves and get coding.
That is not the way a small startup should approach SOA, but if you don't anticipate this it may become an unintended outcome.
We address this separation by working in business-focused teams that have both backend and frontend developers.
SOA is a powerful model for creating scalable software, but many developers are reluctant to adopt it because of this separation myth.
Acknowledging the risk here and architecting your teams with an eye to avoiding it is important in successfully adopting this model in an agile environment.
key_insight: Service-oriented architecture should enable cross-team collaboration rather than create silos, but achieving this requires deliberately designing both your technical architecture and team structure together.
